text.title: Caching Patterns
text.summary: Five caching patterns — cache-aside, read-through, write-through, write-behind, write-around — with use-case rules, avoidance conditions, and a selection matrix keyed to data type and access characteristics.

list.item: Browser: static assets, API responses. Tools: HTTP headers, Service Worker. TTL: 1h–1y.
CDN/Edge: static files, HTML, API responses. Tools: Cloudflare, CloudFront, Fastly. TTL: 5m–24h.
API Gateway: full responses, rate limiting. Tools: Kong, nginx, Envoy. TTL: 1m–1h.
Application: objects, queries, sessions. Tools: Redis, Memcached, in-memory. TTL: 1m–24h.
Database: query results, prepared statements. Tools: built-in query cache. TTL: auto-managed.

rule.statement: Use cache-aside as the default pattern for read-heavy workloads where the application controls cache lifecycle explicitly.
rule.rationale: Application code controls exactly what is cached and when; cache contains only requested data, avoiding prefill waste. Most flexible — compatible with any storage backend.

list.item: Read flow: App → Cache (miss) → DB → Cache (store) → App.
Write flow: App → DB → Cache (invalidate or TTL expiry handles it).
Best for: read-heavy workloads, data that can tolerate staleness, heterogeneous data access patterns.
Avoid for: write-heavy workloads where the cache is constantly invalidated, real-time consistency requirements.

rule.statement: Use read-through when you want transparent cache management — the cache library handles DB fetches on misses, not application code.
rule.rationale: Removes cache-population logic from application code; all reads go through a single path. Simplifies code at the cost of flexibility.

list.item: Read flow: App → Cache (miss) → Cache fetches from DB → App.
Best for: simpler application code, consistent caching logic across all read paths.
Avoid for: complex invalidation requirements, data needing custom transformation before caching.

rule.statement: Use write-through for data that is frequently read immediately after write and where consistency is critical.
rule.rationale: Every write goes to cache and DB synchronously; cache is always warm and consistent. The latency penalty on writes is the trade-off.

list.item: Write flow: App → Cache → DB (synchronous).
Best for: data consistency critical (e.g., shopping cart, user preferences read immediately after update).
Avoid for: write-heavy workloads (latency penalty accumulates), data rarely read after write.

rule.statement: Use write-behind only when eventual consistency is acceptable and write throughput is the primary bottleneck. Never use for financial or inventory data without explicit durability guarantees.
rule.rationale: Async DB persistence risks data loss on cache failure; the throughput gain is only worth it when loss is tolerable.

list.item: Write flow: App → Cache → Queue → DB (asynchronous).
Best for: high write throughput, analytics events, activity logs, eventual consistency acceptable.
Avoid for: critical data (ledger, inventory), strong consistency requirements, systems without a reliable queue to buffer writes.
Write flow: App → DB directly. Cache not updated on write. Reads use cache-aside pattern on subsequent access.
Best for: write-once/read-rarely data, large objects (media files, backups), data unlikely to be re-read soon.
Avoid for: data frequently accessed immediately after write — causes cache misses and DB load spikes.
User profiles → Cache-Aside + TTL: read-heavy, tolerate staleness.
Shopping cart → Write-Through: consistency critical, read immediately after write.
Analytics events → Write-Behind: high volume, eventual consistency acceptable.
File uploads → Write-Around: large, rarely re-read soon.
Session data → Read-Through: transparent management, high hit rate.
Leaderboards → Cache-Aside + Event invalidation: frequent reads, periodic updates.
Configuration/feature flags → Cache-Aside + short TTL: rarely changes, safe to serve slightly stale.
Auth lookups → Cache-Aside + short TTL: high frequency, low staleness tolerance.
